segments.purgatorio-18-g1: Posto (past participle of porre) precedes the imperfect auxiliary avea to form a literary pluperfect (avea posto = 'had put'); fine al suo ragionamento is the direct object meaning 'an end to his reasoning'. L’alto dottore is the subject placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-18-g2: E introduces the next clause. Attento guardava uses the imperfect guardare ('was watching') with attento as adverbial/attribute 'attentively'. Ne la mia vista = 'in my expression/face' (ne = in/within). S’io is contraction of se io ('if/whether I'); parea is imperfect 'seemed'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g3: Simple coordinate particle e plus the first-person pronoun io ('and I'), marking the speaker as subject for the following clauses.
segments.purgatorio-18-g4: Cui is a dative/relative pronoun here ('to whom' / 'for whom'); nova sete = 'a new thirst' (poetic image). Ancor = 'still/yet'. Frugava is the imperfect of frugare 'to search/gnaw at' (metaphorical 'was searching/gnawing').
segments.purgatorio-18-g5: Di fuor ('from outside', i.e. 'externally' or 'outwardly') modifies tacea, the imperfect of tacere 'to be silent' — 'I was silent on the outside.' Word order is inverted for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-18-g6: E dentro ('and within/inside') contrasts with di fuor; dicea is the imperfect of dire ('was saying' / 'I said inwardly'), indicating inner speech in the past.
segments.purgatorio-18-g7: Forse = 'perhaps'. Lo is the neuter article/pronoun pointing to the idea 'the too much asking'. Troppo dimandar is a nominalized infinitive 'asking too much'. Ch’ = che 'that'. Io fo is the archaic present 'I do/make' (fo = faccio). Li is a pronoun here meaning 'them' (not listed among the provided senses); grava is present 'weighs down/burdens'. The whole clause is an inward conjecture.
segments.purgatorio-18-g8: Ma = 'but' / 'yet' introducing contrast. Quel is the demonstrative 'that'. Padre here is an honorific 'father' (spiritual/teacher) and verace is an adjective 'truthful' or 'genuine'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g9: Che introduces a relative clause. S’accorse is the passato remoto (si accorse) meaning 'he perceived/realized' (s’ = si, reflexive). Del = di + il 'of the'. Timido voler = 'timid will/wish' (voler as noun). Che non s’apriva is a subordinate relative 'which would not open/reveal itself' (apriva imperfect of aprirsi, reflexive).
segments.purgatorio-18-g10: Parlando is a gerund ('speaking') used adverbially to indicate manner or simultaneously with the main action.
segments.purgatorio-18-g11: Di parlare ardir mi porse: di + infinitive (parlare) introduces the noun ardir 'the daring/boldness' as object; mi is the indirect object 'to me'; porse is passato remoto of porgere 'he offered/held out'. The sequence means 'he offered me the boldness to speak.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g12: Ond’ is a contracted form of onde/ond, functioning as 'whereupon' / 'therefore'; io is the speaker's pronoun 'I' initiating direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-18-g13: Maestro is the vocative 'Master' (addressing his teacher/guide). It stands alone as the opening of direct address.
segments.purgatorio-18-g14: Il mio veder is a noun phrase 'my seeing' / 'my sight' (il + possessive + infinitive used nominally). S’avviva is reflexive present (si avviva) meaning 'is enlivened/revived' (third-person singular present). Sì = 'so/so much'; nel tuo lume = 'in your light' (metaphorical).
segments.purgatorio-18-g15: Ch’ introduces a result/subordinate clause 'that'. Io discerno is present indicative 'I discern/ perceive'. Chiaro modifies the verb 'clearly'. Quanto introduces an indirect question 'how much/to what degree'; la tua ragion is the subject of the subordinate verbs. Parta and descriva are subjunctive forms (present) used in the indirect clause after quanto, meaning 'depart' / 'describe' respectively — two possibilities contrasted by o 'or'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g16: Però = 'therefore' / 'then' introducing the petition; ti prego is first-person present 'I beg you' (prego used as 'I ask/implore').
segments.purgatorio-18-g17: Dolce padre caro is a series of address adjectives: 'sweet, dear father' — two affectionate adjectives modifying padre in vocative position.
segments.purgatorio-18-g18: Che introduces the content of the petition: mi dimostri amore. Dimostri is present subjunctive (a polite/requestive subjunctive after prego) or a poetic use of present; it means 'you show me' or 'may you show me', and amore is the direct object (here personified 'Love').
segments.purgatorio-18-g19: A cui = 'to whom' (dative relative), reduci is present indicative 2nd-person singular (tu reduci) 'you reduce/bring back/bring under the principle of' in the sense 'you reduce to (that principle)'. Ogne is an archaic form of ogni 'every'; buono operare = 'good action/doing' (operare as noun); e ’l suo contraro = 'and its opposite' (’l = il). The clause assigns to Love the role of to whom all good deeds and their opposites are reduced/interpreted.
segments.purgatorio-18-g20: Drizza is an imperative, 2nd‑person singular, from drizzare (old/poetic). It commands to 'direct/turn/raise' something toward the speaker; the direct object is supplied by the following phrase.
segments.purgatorio-18-g21: disse is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd‑person singular of dire, used here as the narrative 'he said' introducing the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-18-g22: ver' is a contracted form of verso (toward) used adverbially; me is the clitic indirect object 'to me'; l'agute luci is the feminine plural noun phrase 'the sharp/keen lights' (agute agrees with luci); de lo 'ntelletto is a genitive-of-relationship 'of the intellect' with the archaic elision of the initial i in 'intelletto (written 'ntelletto). Word order has the directional phrase (ver' me) before the object it affects (l'agute luci).
segments.purgatorio-18-g23: This clause begins with the conjunction e 'and'. fieti is an archaic/poetic form (not modern) that functions to indicate the result 'will be to you / will become to you' (Dante often uses contracted/inverted futures here); manifesto is predicate adjective 'manifest/evident' and l'error de' ciechi is the subject 'the error of the blind'. che si fanno duci is a relative clause 'who make themselves leaders', with si reflecting a reflexive 'make themselves' and duci as a plural predicate noun. (Because fieti is an archaic form, modern editions render it as something like 'ti farà' or 'si farà manifesto a te'.)
segments.purgatorio-18-g24: L' is the elided definite article (il) and animo is the noun 'soul / mind'; the comma sets off the following relative clause modifying this subject.
segments.purgatorio-18-g25: ch' is the relative conjunction che 'which/that'; è is the present indicative of essere 'is'; creato is a past participle used adjectivally 'created/made'; ad + infinitive amar means 'made to love' (ad = 'to/for'), and presto is an adverb 'quickly' or 'easily/soon' describing the manner of loving. The whole clause gives a defining property of 'animo'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g26: ad ogne cosa = 'toward every thing' (ad + ogne 'every' + cosa 'thing'); è mobile is a predicative expression 'is movable/liable to be moved' (mobile = changeable/movable); che piace is a relative clause 'that pleases' (piace is the verb 'to please'). The construction says the soul is easily moved toward anything that pleases it.
segments.purgatorio-18-g27: tosto che is a temporal conjunction meaning 'as soon as' (tosto = immediately/soon); dal (da + il) = 'from the'; piacere here = 'pleasure'; in atto = 'into action' or 'in act' (atto = act); è desto is 'is awake/roused' (desto = awake). Grammatically this gives the time when the soul becomes moved.
segments.purgatorio-18-g28: Vostra is the possessive 'your'. apprensiva here functions as a substantive or abstract noun from apprensire — 'apprehension' or 'perception' (the sense of grasping). da esser verace is an infinitival phrase 'in being/considering itself true' (da used in this sense = 'in/as'), and tragge (3rd singular present of trarre in older form) means 'draws' or 'derives'. intenzione is the direct object 'intention/purpose'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g29: e = 'and'; dentro a voi = 'within you' (dentro + a + voi); la is the direct object pronoun 'it/her' referring back to intenzione; spiega is from spiegare and here means 'unfolds / explains / makes clear'. The phrase says the apprehension unfolds that intention inside you.
segments.purgatorio-18-g30: sì che = 'so (that)' introducing a result clause; l'animo = 'the soul/mind' (subject); ad essa = 'toward it' (essa = that feminine thing just mentioned); volger is the infinitive 'to turn' and face is an older/poetic form of fare (3rd singular subjunctive/imperative nuance) functioning causatively here: 'causes to turn' or 'makes to turn'. The clause expresses that the result of the explanation is to make the soul turn toward the intention.
segments.purgatorio-18-g31: e se = 'and if' (se is the conditional/if conjunction); the segment is the protasis marker introducing the conditional clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-18-g32: rivolto is a past participle (from rivolgere) used adjectivally: 'turned' or 'directed' (here describing the state of the soul once it has been turned toward the object).
segments.purgatorio-18-g33: inver' is a contracted/poetic prepositional/adverbial form (here used to indicate orientation toward) — the exact lexical entry is archaic; di lei = 'toward her/it' (of her), si is the reflexive pronoun 'itself/himself', and piega is present indicative of piegare 'bends/leans/turns'. Together, the clause describes the soul's leaning or turning toward the object (lei).
segments.purgatorio-18-g34: quel piegare = 'that bending/leaning' (quel = that; piegare as noun/gerundive idea); è amor = 'is love' (predicate identifying the leaning as love). The clause equates the motion/turning with love.
segments.purgatorio-18-g35: quell' è natura = 'that is nature' (identifying the previous fact as natural); che per piacer di novo = relative clause 'which, through renewed pleasure' (per = through/by means of; piacer = pleasure; di novo = anew/again); in voi si lega = 'is bound in you' (si lega is reflexive present 'binds itself to you' or 'becomes bound in you'). The final clause explains this as a natural binding produced by renewed pleasure.
segments.purgatorio-18-g36: Poi is an adverbial discourse marker placed sentence-initial meaning "then/afterwards." It sets the temporal or logical sequence for the simile that follows.
segments.purgatorio-18-g37: This whole clause is a simile introduced by come ("as"). l' foco is the subject (the article l' before foco). movesi is 3rd-person singular present with the enclitic reflexive si written as one word ("moves/sets itself in motion"); in altura indicates upward direction. per + noun (per la sua forma) gives the principle or cause ("by/through its form"). ch'è nata a salire is a relative clause: ch' = che "which," è = "is," nata is a past participle agreeing with forma (feminine) meaning "born/arisen," and a salire (infinitive) expresses the inherent tendency "to rise." là dove ... dura is an adverbial relative "there where," with dura an adjective modifying matera ("matter") — the inverted word order is poetic.
segments.purgatorio-18-g38: Così "thus/so" links the simile to the human case. l'animo preso: preso is a past participle used adjectivally meaning "seized/possessed," following the noun; entra is 3rd-person singular present "enters," and in disire uses the archaic spelling disire for the noun "desire." The phrase means the soul, once seized, enters into desire.
segments.purgatorio-18-g39: Ch' è is the contraction of che è ("which is"). moto is a noun here meaning "motion/impulse," and spiritale is an adjective "spiritual," forming the nominal phrase "a spiritual motion." The relative clause identifies the nature of the desire.
segments.purgatorio-18-g40: E mai non posa contains a double-negative construction (mai non) used to mean "never," with posa (3rd-person present) meaning "rests/ceases" (not "place"). Fin che introduces a temporal subordinate clause "until" (fin che + indicative here). la cosa amata is "the thing loved" (amata is a past participle agreeing with cosa). Il is a clitic pronoun referring back to l'animo (masculine) and is the object of fa ("makes"); gioire is the infinitive complement to fare ("to rejoice"), so la cosa amata il fa gioire = "the beloved thing makes it/him rejoice."
segments.purgatorio-18-g78: Onde is a connective adverb meaning 'therefore' or 'from which', used here to introduce a conclusion drawn from the preceding lines. It is an archaic/poetic linking word rather than a locative.
segments.purgatorio-18-g79: Poniam is an exhortative/ hortatory form (early/poetic Italian) meaning 'let us suppose' and governs the subordinate clause introduced by che. Di necessitate = 'from/by necessity' (di marking origin). Surga is present subjunctive (3rd sg.) of surgere ('arise') used because the clause is put as a hypothesis after poniam; the verb precedes its noun (surga ogne amor) for poetic inversion. Che dentro a voi s'accende is a relative clause 'which is kindled within you', with s'accende a contraction of si accende (reflexive/passive sense 'is kindled').
segments.purgatorio-18-g80: Di ritenerlo is an infinitive phrase (ritenerlo = ritenere + lo 'it') placed before the verb by poetic inversion; it specifies what the 'podestate' is for ('the power of restraining it'). È in voi la podestate is the main clause: è = 'is', in voi = 'in you' (locative), la podestate = 'the power/authority' (subject after inversion).
segments.purgatorio-18-g81: La nobile virtù Beatrice is a nominal phrase where 'virtù' (virtue/faculty) is personified and apposed to the proper name Beatrice. Intende is 3rd person singular present ('understands/means') and per lo libero arbitrio uses per + article + noun to indicate 'with/by/through free will' (per here = 'in terms of' or 'by means of'). The article lo before libero is the expected medieval usage.
segments.purgatorio-18-g82: E però guarda is an admonition: e però = 'and therefore', guarda here functions as an imperative ('be careful' / 'take care'). Che introduces a subordinate clause with the present subjunctive abbi ('that you have it in mind')—l' is the clitic 'lo' ('it') referring back to the preceding idea, and a mente = 'in mind'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g83: S'a parlar ten prende is an archaic phrasing meaning roughly 'if she begins/sets herself to speak'. s' is a contraction related to se/si ('if' / reflexive), a = 'to', parlar = infinitive 'to speak', and prende ('prendere') here has the sense 'begin, take up' (a common poetic use). Ten is an archaic form (see comment) used with prende in this periphrasis 'ten/prende' = 'takes to' (modern Italian would have tien prende / si prende).
segments.purgatorio-18-g84: La is the definite feminine singular article placed before the noun; luna is the feminine singular noun 'moon' serving as the subject of the sentence. The comma isolates the subject for the descriptive clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-18-g85: quasi here means 'almost' / 'as if'. a mezza notte is a prepositional phrase ('at the middle of the night'): mezza is the feminine form of mezzo 'middle/half' and agrees with notte. tarda is an adjective 'late' agreeing in gender and number with notte and placed after it in idiomatic word order.
segments.purgatorio-18-g86: facea is the imperfect (archaic/poetic spelling for faceva) of fare used causatively: 'made/caused' + infinitive. le stelle is the feminine plural direct object ('the stars'); a noi parer places the indirect object a noi ('to us') before the infinitive parer (older form of parere 'to seem/appear'). più rade is a comparative/adverbial phrase ('more sparse'), with rade agreeing in gender and number with stelle.
segments.purgatorio-18-g87: fatta is the past participle (feminine singular) agreeing with la luna and here functioning as a predicative adjective ('made/appearing like'); com' is a contraction of come 'as/like'. un secchion is a simile 'a bookworm' (secchion = studious/bookish person). che introduces a relative clause; tuttor is an archaic form meaning 'always'; arda is a present-subjunctive/poetic form of ardere ('burn') used in older/poetic Italian (modern indicative would be arde), so the clause describes the habitual characteristic of that bookworm.
segments.purgatorio-18-g88: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. correa is an archaic/imperfect form of the verb correre ('ran, was running') in third person singular; here it gives motion across the sky. contro 'l ciel = contro + el (contracted to 'l) + ciel, literally 'against the sky' or 'across the sky'. per quelle strade uses per to indicate motion along/through 'those paths'. che introduces a relative clause modifying strade: 'which the sun infiamma' (infiamma is present indicative 'sets on fire/ inflames'). allor che = 'when'. The sequence quel da Roma tra 'Sardi e 'Corsi il vede quando cade has inverted word order typical of poetry: quel 'that one' (subject) 'da Roma' 'seen/from Rome', 'tra Sardi e Corsi' 'between Sardinians and Corsicans', il vede 'sees it' (il = object pronoun referring back), quando cade 'when it falls/sets.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g89: E = 'and' linking this clause to what came before. 'quell' + 'ombra gentil' is a demonstrative + noun phrase ('that gentle/noble shade'). 'per cui' introduces a relative clause meaning 'by/for whom'; here 'si' is the impersonal/passive reflexive used with the verb 'noma' to mean 'is called' (an archaic or dialectal form of nominative/nomare). 'Pietola più che villa mantoana' is the complement of 'si noma' (literally 'Pietola more than Mantuan villa'), with 'più che' functioning as 'rather than/more than' to mark the preferred name. The line breaks and inversion are poetic; the head noun 'ombra' is what the relative phrase modifies.
segments.purgatorio-18-g90: 'del mio carcar' = 'of/from my carcer/prison' with 'del' = di + il. 'carcar' is an archaic form related to 'carcere' (prison); poetic diction often uses such forms. 'diposta avea la soma' is an example of inverted periphrasis common in Dante: the past participle 'diposta' (disposed/laid aside) precedes the auxiliary 'avea' (had), so 'avea diposta' = 'had taken away/put aside' and 'la soma' (the load/burden) is the direct object; 'diposta' agrees in gender/number with 'soma' (feminine).
segments.purgatorio-18-g91: 'per ch’ io' = 'per che io' is a compressed, archaic/conjunctive sequence meaning 'wherefore/so I' or 'because/therefore I'; 'per' here has a causal/relative force and 'ch’' is the elided form of 'che'. The phrase starts a consequence clause leading into the following relative clause about the speaker's mental state.
segments.purgatorio-18-g92: The relative 'che' ('who/which') introduces a descriptive clause about the subject 'io'. 'la ragione' is the noun 'reason/understanding'; the adjectives 'aperta e piana' follow it and mean 'open and plain/clear' (postnominal adjectives are common in poetry). 'sovra le mie quistioni' is a prepositional phrase 'about/with respect to my questions', and 'avea ricolta' is the pluperfect periphrasis (auxiliary 'avea' + past participle 'ricolta'), here 'had gathered/collected'—so the whole clause: 'I, who had gathered clear and plain reason about my questions...'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g93: 'stava' is the imperfect of 'stare' used to mean 'stood/was standing' (a continuous past). 'com' = 'come' (as/like) introduces the simile 'as a man'. 'om' is an archaic form of 'uom'/'uomo' (man). The relative 'che' ('who') introduces the subordinate quality 'sonnolento vana': here Dante uses an elliptical, compressed phrasing—'sonnolento' = 'sleepy/drowsy' and 'vana' = 'vain/futile'; grammatically there is a feminine adjective where one expects masculine agreement, which indicates an implicit feminine noun (for example 'sonnolenza' = 'drowsiness') or poetic license. The sense is 'stood like a man whose sleepiness is vain/fruitless.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g147: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Quella is a feminine demonstrative pronoun 'the one' / 'she' introducing a relative clause begun by che 'who/which'. In the relative clause l’affanno is the noun with the definite article (the suffering/anguish) positioned before the verb for poetic emphasis; non sofferse is the passato remoto, 3rd person singular of soffrire with negation (literally 'did not endure'/'did not suffer'); fino a la fine is an adverbial phrase 'until the end' and col (con + il) figlio d'Anchise is a prepositional phrase 'with the son of Anchises' modifying fino alla fine (i.e. 'to the end with Anchises' son').
segments.purgatorio-18-g148: Sé stessa is a reflexive emphatic object 'herself' (sé + stessa). A vita means 'to/a life' (offered herself to a life); sanza is the archaic form of senza 'without'; gloria means 'glory' (praise/heavenly bliss). Offerse is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of the archaic offerso/offrire here meaning 'offered' — together the phrase says she 'offered herself to a life without glory'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g149: Poi 'then' / 'afterward'. Quando 'when' introduces the temporal clause. Fuor is an archaic/poetic form related to fuori 'out'/'were out' indicating separation; da noi 'from us'. Tanto divise means 'so separated' or 'so far apart' (tanto intensifies divise 'separated'). Quell' ombre (those shades) is the postponed subject, typical poetic word order.
segments.purgatorio-18-g150: Che is a relative conjunction 'that' referring back to 'ombre'. Veder is the infinitive 'to see' used impersonally here; più non potiersi is an older impersonal/passive construction with potersi (poter + si), meaning 'could no longer be' (i.e. 'were no longer able to be seen') — più = 'any longer'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g151: Novo (archaic nuovo) 'new' modifies pensiero 'thought'. Dentro a me 'within me' (dentro + a + me) locates the thought internally. Si mise is the reflexive past (mettersi) in passato remoto, 3rd person singular; literally 'set itself' or idiomatically 'entered/took hold' — 'a new thought arose within me'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g152: Del qual 'of which' (relative reference to the new thought). Più altri 'more others' = 'many other(s)' (più as an intensifier). Nacquero is passato remoto, 3rd person plural 'arose/were born'. E diversi adds that those others were various/different; the whole phrase: 'from which many other and different [thoughts] arose.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g153: E 'and'. Tanto 'so much' intensifies the following. D’uno in altro is a contracted prepositional phrase 'from one into another' (di + uno ... in altro) describing the movement of thought. Vaneggiai is passato remoto, 1st person singular of vaneggiare 'I raved'/'I fancied' — 'I wandered in fancy from one thing to another.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g154: Che introduces the result clause ('so that'). Li occhi 'the eyes' (definite article + noun) are the grammatical subject of ricopersi here. Per vaghezza: per = 'through/because of', vaghezza in this context means attraction/longing/desire. Ricopersi is the past reflexive form (ricoprirsi) in passato remoto, 3rd person plural 'were covered' / 'closed over themselves' — i.e. 'my eyes were covered/closed because of longing.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g155: E 'and'. ’L (il) pensamento 'the thought' is the direct object/topic. In sogno 'into a dream' marks the result or transformation. Trasmutai is passato remoto, 1st person singular of trasmutare 'I transmuted/changed' — 'and I changed the thought into a dream.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g64: Però is a sentence-linking adverb meaning 'therefore' or 'then'; here it introduces the consequence or conclusion that follows. It stands alone as a short emphatic lead-in.
segments.purgatorio-18-g65: This clause is a locative/relative phrase: là (there) + onde (whence/from which) + vegna (vegnare, archaic/poetic form meaning 'comes' or 'arrives') + lo 'ntelletto ('the intellect') + de le prime notizie ('of the first notions'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect; 'vegna' is a present/poetic form meaning 'comes' or 'is derived'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g66: omo (man) + non (negation) + sape (archaic past form of sapere) together mean 'man did not know'. In Dante this short present-looking form functions as a past/simple narrative ('knew'/'did know'); the negation 'non' directly precedes the verb.
segments.purgatorio-18-g67: e (and) + de' primi appetibili l'affetto = 'and the affection of the first desirable things.' The preposition 'de' (= di) marks the genitive/relationship, 'primi' modifies 'appetibili' ('first desirable [things]'), and l'affetto is the emotional response or 'feeling' toward them.
segments.purgatorio-18-g68: This relative clause explains that those appetites 'are in you' (sono in voi) 'as' (come) 'studio in ape di far lo mele' — 'studio' here means zeal or industriousness; 'in ape' = 'in a bee'; 'di far lo mele' = 'to make the honey' (far = infinitive 'to make'). Word order is simile-like: X sono in voi sì come Y in Z.
segments.purgatorio-18-g69: e (and) + questa prima voglia (this first desire) + merto di lode o di biasmo non cape. Here 'merto' = 'merit' (desert, claim to reward), while 'cape' is an archaic/unusual form that in context functions as a verb meaning 'takes'/'accepts' (from capere); Dante uses it negatively with 'non' to mean 'does not receive/accept'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g70: Or (now) introduces the sentence; perché here means 'because'. The phrase 'a questa ogn' altra si raccoglia' is inverted: 'to this every other gathers' — 'si' is the reflexive/impersonal pronoun making 'raccoglia' intransitive ('is gathered' / 'gathers itself').
segments.purgatorio-18-g71: innata (innate) + v'è (vi è = 'there is') + la virtù (the virtue) + che consiglia (that advises). The inversion 'innata v'è la virtù' is poetic: 'there is an innate virtue' with the relative clause in present indicative describing its action ('consiglia').
segments.purgatorio-18-g72: e (and) + de l'assenso (of the assent/consent) + de' tener la soglia. Here 'de' (= di) links 'assenso' to the infinitive 'tener' ('to keep/hold'); 'la soglia' ('the threshold') is the object of 'tener'. Grammatically this gives 'and the consent to keep the threshold.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g73: Quest' è 'l principio (This is the principle) + là onde (therefrom/from which) + si piglia (si + piglia, reflexive/impersonal 'is taken' or 'one takes') + ragion di meritare in voi ('the reason/judgment about deserving in you'). 'Si piglia' is a passive/reflexive construction meaning 'is derived/taken.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g74: secondo che (according as/depending on) + buoni e rei amori (good and guilty loves) + accoglie e viglia. 'Accoglie' is present indicative 'welcomes/accepts.' 'Viglia' here is an archaic present form of 'vigilare' meaning 'watches over' or 'guards'; Dante uses both verbs to say the principle judges according to how these loves are received and kept.
segments.purgatorio-18-g75: Color che (those who) + ragionando (gerund/participle 'reasoning') + andaro (archaic past plural 'they went') + al fondo ('to the bottom/depth'). The participle 'ragionando' modifies the subject; 'andaro' is Dante's older past-tense form (modern 'andarono').
segments.purgatorio-18-g76: s'accorser (contracted si + accorser) is an archaic past form meaning 'they perceived/realized' (modern 'si accorsero'). d'esta innata libertate = 'of this innate liberty' (d' = di). The pronominal 's'' is part of the full past verb form.
segments.purgatorio-18-g77: però (therefore) links the consequence: moralità (morality/proper moral conduct) + lasciaro (archaic past 'they left' = modern 'lasciarono') + al mondo ('to the world'). Together: 'therefore they left morality to the world.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g113: This is a vocative opening addressing 'O gente' (oh/people). 'in cui' begins a relative clause 'in whom'; 'fervore aguto adesso' is a noun phrase meaning 'a keen/acute fervor now' (aguto is poetic/archaic). 'ricompie' is a present indicative meaning 'fills again / takes the place of' and governs the following objects 'negligenza e indugio'. 'da voi' marks the agent/source ('by you' / 'on your part'), and 'per tepidezza in ben far messo' gives the reason: 'because of lukewarmness in being set to do good'. The word order is typical Dantean Italian: relative + predicate + objects + agent + causal phrase.
segments.purgatorio-18-g114: 'questi che vive' is a short clause pointing to 'this one who lives' (questi = 'this one; he'). 'che' is the relative pronoun 'who' and 'vive' is the present indicative 'lives' — a simple identification of the person being spoken about.
segments.purgatorio-18-g115: The speaker appends 'e certo i’ non vi bugio' to insist on truthfulness. 'e certo' = 'and certainly'; 'i’' is the poetic/apostrophized form of 'io' ('I') and 'non vi bugio' = 'I do not deceive you' (an emphatic denial). The clause is parenthetical and confirms the sincerity of the statement.
segments.purgatorio-18-g116: 'vuole andar sù' is a simple finite clause: 'he wants to go up.' 'vuole' is third-person singular present of volere; 'andar' is the infinitive 'to go;' 'sù' is the adverb 'up'. The subject is 'questi' from the previous segment.
segments.purgatorio-18-g117: 'pur che' is a concessive/conditional particle here meaning 'provided that' or 'so long as.' '’l sol' is the poetic contraction for 'il sole' ('the sun') and 'ne riluca' uses the pronominal 'ne' referring back to the person ('on him' / 'upon him') with 'riluca' (present indicative of rilucere) 'shines forth / gleams.' The whole line gives the condition under which he may go up.
segments.purgatorio-18-g118: 'però' ('therefore') introduces the request that follows. 'ne dite' is an imperative or polite request 'tell us of it' (ne = 'of it / about it'); 'ond’ è presso il pertugio' is a fused clause 'from where (ond' = 'whence') it is near the opening' — i.e. 'tell us whence is the opening that is near.' The syntax asks for the location of the hole/opening.
segments.purgatorio-18-g119: 'Parole furon queste del mio duca' is a past-stative construction: 'these were the words of my duca.' 'furon' is the literary past (third-person plural) of essere used here as 'were'; 'del mio duca' identifies the speaker being quoted (my duca = my guide). Word order places the verb before the demonstrative for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-18-g120: 'e un di quelli spirti disse:' is straightforward narration: 'and one of those spirits said.' 'un di quelli' = 'one of those,' 'spirti' (spiriti spelled without the second i) = 'spirits/souls,' and 'disse' is the simple past 'said.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g121: The quoted imperative 'Vieni di retro a noi' = 'Come behind us.' 'Vieni' here is an imperative/encouraging form ('come!'); 'di retro' = 'from behind' or 'behind'; 'a noi' = 'to us' or 'with us.' The phrase invites the addressee to fall in behind the speakers.
segments.purgatorio-18-g122: 'e troverai la buca.' is the future indicative 'and you will find the hole.' 'troverai' is second-person singular future of trovare; 'la buca' is the direct object 'the hole/opening.' It continues the imperative invitation with the promise of what will be encountered.
segments.purgatorio-18-g123: 'Noi siam di voglia a muoverci sì pieni' means 'we are so full of desire to move ourselves.' 'Noi siam' = 'we are' (siam = siamo in archaic form); 'di voglia' = 'of desire/will'; 'a muoverci' = 'to set ourselves in motion'; 'sì pieni' = 'so full.' The adjective follows and intensifies the preceding noun phrase.
segments.purgatorio-18-g124: 'che restar non potem' is an explanatory subordinate: 'that we cannot remain.' 'che' introduces the result/clause; 'restar' is the infinitive 'to remain'; 'non' negates; 'potem' is an archaic form of 'possiamo' (we can). The phrase means they cannot stay where they are.
segments.purgatorio-18-g125: 'però perdona' is an appeal: 'therefore forgive (us).' 'però' = 'therefore/so'; 'perdona' is either an imperative 'forgive' or a present indicative of 'perdonare' used politely. It asks pardon for the forthcoming movement.
segments.purgatorio-18-g126: 'se villania nostra giustizia tieni' is a concessive conditional: 'if you hold our rudeness to be justice.' 'se' = 'if'; 'villania nostra' = 'our rudeness / rude behaviour'; 'giustizia tieni' = literally 'you hold as justice' — 'tieni' (you hold/consider). The clause asks forgiveness should the behaviour seem rude to the listener.
segments.purgatorio-18-g127: 'Io fui abate in San Zeno a Verona sotto lo ’mperio del buon Barbarossa' is a biographical statement in the past. 'Io fui' = 'I was' (fui = literary past of essere); 'abate' = 'abbot' (title); 'in San Zeno a Verona' gives the church and city; 'sotto lo ’mperio del buon Barbarossa' = 'under the empire/rule of the good Barbarossa' (Barbarossa = Frederick I). Word order is typical: subject + past verb + complement details.
segments.purgatorio-18-g128: 'di cui dolente ancor Milan ragiona' is a relative clause giving a contemporary comment: 'of whom a sorrowful Milan still speaks.' 'di cui' = 'of whom'; 'dolente' = 'sorrowful' (describing Milan's feeling); 'ancor' = 'still'; 'Milan ragiona' = 'Milan speaks/talks (about him).' The verb ragiona is present, indicating the continued memory of Barbarossa's deeds.
segments.purgatorio-18-g94: A single clause in the perfect-past passive: 'Ma' = but; 'questa sonnolenza' is the feminine subject ('this drowsiness'); 'mi fu tolta' is a compound past passive (fu = auxiliary from essere + tolta, past participle of togliere) meaning 'was taken away from me'; 'subitamente' is an adverb modifying the verb; 'da gente' marks the agent with da ('by people'); the relative clause 'che dopo le nostre spalle a noi era già volta' (literally 'who after our shoulders to us was already turned') uses an impersonal/archaic word order – 'dopo le nostre spalle' = 'behind our backs/shoulders', 'a noi' = 'toward us', and 'era già volta' is the pluperfect/imperfect sense of 'to turn' (here 3rd sing, 'had already turned').
segments.purgatorio-18-g95: 'E' = and; 'quale' = 'such as' (introducing a simile); 'Ismeno già vide e Asopo' = 'Ismenus and Asopus once saw' where 'già' has the sense 'formerly/once' and 'vide' is the simple past tense (poetic/historical past). 'lungo di sè' = 'along their own [banks]' with 'sé' reflexive referring to the rivers; 'di notte' = 'at night'; 'furia e calca' names two phenomena: 'furia' (fury/violent excitement) and 'calca' (a crowd/press or trampling), the second word is from calcare and denotes pressing/trampling here.
segments.purgatorio-18-g96: 'pur che' is a concessive/conditional connector here meaning 'provided that' or 'whenever'; 'i Teban' = 'the Thebans' (i = article); 'di Bacco' = 'of Bacchus' (i.e., in connection with Bacchic rites); 'avesser uopo' is an archaic subjunctive/imperfect form (avesser ≈ avessero) + 'uopo' (need) meaning 'should have need' or 'needed'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g97: 'cotal' = 'such'; 'per quel giron suo' = 'through that circle of his' ('giron' = circle, suo = his/its); 'passo' = 'passage' or 'course'; 'falca' is a poetic verb (from falcare/falcare) meaning roughly 'reaps/cuts through' or 'makes its way'—the clause says that the throng cuts/makes passage through that circle.
segments.purgatorio-18-g98: 'per quel ch’io vidi di color' = 'by/according to what I saw of those [people]'; 'per quel che' is an idiom meaning 'from/according to that which'; 'ch’io vidi' is the relative clause 'that I saw' (ch' = che); 'di color' = 'of those ones' (color is an archaic plural demonstrative).
segments.purgatorio-18-g99: Single gerund 'venendo' = 'coming' (present gerund), linking the previous observation to their approach: 'those people, coming...'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g100: 'cui' = 'upon whom' (relative pronoun dative/locative sense); 'buon volere e giusto amor' = 'good will and righteous love' (two nouns joined by 'e'); 'cavalca' is 3rd person singular present of 'cavalcare' = 'rides' or 'are astride/borne upon'. Grammatically the verb appears singular though two nouns precede it; poetically they form a single combined force 'good will and righteous love' that 'ride' those people.
segments.purgatorio-18-g101: 'Tosto' = quickly/soon; 'fur' is the archaic past plural of essere (fur = were/they were) used here poetically; 'sovr’' is contraction of 'sovra' = 'over/upon'; 'a noi' = 'upon us' (indirect object), so 'they quickly were upon us' = 'they quickly came upon us'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g102: 'perché' = 'because' giving the cause; 'correndo' = gerund 'running'; 'si movea' = imperfect reflexive 'was moving itself' (3rd sing) meaning 'was in motion'; 'tutta quella turba magna' = 'that whole great crowd' (tutta + quella + noun + adjective order is normal in poetry).
segments.purgatorio-18-g103: 'e due dinanzi' = 'and two in front' (dinanzi = ahead/in front); 'gridavan' = imperfect plural 'were crying/shouting'; 'piangendo' = gerund 'weeping', describing how they cried out.
segments.purgatorio-18-g104: Direct speech: 'Maria corse' uses the past historic (3rd sg) 'corse' = 'ran/hurried'; 'con fretta' = 'with haste'; 'a la montagna' = 'to the mountain' (a + article + noun).
segments.purgatorio-18-g105: Simple connective and name: 'e' = and; 'Cesare' = Caesar (subject of the next clause).
segments.purgatorio-18-g106: 'per' introduces purpose 'in order to'; 'soggiogare' is the infinitive 'to subjugate'; 'Ilerda' is the place/object. Together: 'to subjugate Ilerda.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g107: 'punse' is the aoristic/past tense of 'pungere' (literally 'pricked' but here used figuratively = 'struck/attacked'); 'Marsilia' = Marseille (object); 'e poi corse in Ispagna' = 'and then rushed into Spain' where 'corse' is again the simple past 'ran/forced his way' and 'in Ispagna' = 'into Spain.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g108: Exclamatory single word 'Ratto' used as an interjection/adverb meaning 'Quick!' or 'Swiftly!'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g109: Repeated 'ratto' for emphasis ('Quick, quick'). Same function as the previous word.
segments.purgatorio-18-g110: 'che' introduces a purpose/result clause here: 'so that'; '’l tempo' = 'il tempo' (the time); 'non si perda' uses present subjunctive 'perda' with reflexive 'si' as an impersonal/passive construction meaning 'may/let the time not be lost' or 'that time not be wasted'; 'per poco amor' = 'through/because of little/slight love.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g111: 'gridavan' = imperfect 'were shouting/crying out'; 'li altri' = 'the others' (old order with clitic/article 'li'); 'appresso' = 'after them' or 'nearby/in response', indicating they called after the two who spoke.
segments.purgatorio-18-g112: 'che' here functions as 'for/because' introducing the explanation; 'studio' = zeal/effort (literally 'study' but in this idiom = eagerness or zeal); 'di ben far' = 'of doing well' i.e. 'to do good'; 'grazia' = favor/grace; 'rinverda' = present tense 'revives/renews' (from rinverdir/ rinverdare, literally 'makes green again') — the clause states the reason: zeal for doing good renews grace/favor.
segments.purgatorio-18-g129: E tale ha già l’un piè dentro la fossa, is a single clause: the coordinating conjunction E (‘and’) begins the clause; tale is a demonstrative adjective meaning “such (a one).” Ha (present indicative of avere) functions as a full verb meaning “has” and takes the direct object l’un piè (“one foot”) with già (“already”) marking that the condition is already true. Dentro la fossa is a prepositional phrase ‘inside the pit/grave’ that completes the image; the archaic article contraction l’ before un is simply a poetic shortening.
segments.purgatorio-18-g130: che tosto piangerà quel monastero, begins with the relative/causal che linking this result to the previous image; tosto is an adverb meaning “soon,” and piangerà is future indicative of piangere, here meaning “will lament/weep.” Quel monastero (‘that monastery’) is the subject that will do the lamenting; word order (verb before subject) is common in poetry and emphasizes the impending action.
segments.purgatorio-18-g131: e tristo fia d’avere avuta possa; uses tristo (adjective) with the future auxiliary fia (from essere: will be) to say “it will be sad/mournful.” The phrase d’avere avuta possa is a noun phrase governed by di meaning roughly “to have had power/authority”: avere (infinitive) + avuta (past participle used substantively) + possa (here a substantive ‘power’ from potere). The whole phrase says the monastery will rue having possessed that power/authority.
segments.purgatorio-18-g132: perché suo figlio, is the causal conjunction perché ‘because’ introducing the explanation; suo is a possessive modifying figlio (‘son’), here referring back to the community’s own member or protégé. This short segment sets up the reasons that follow.
segments.purgatorio-18-g133: mal del corpo intero, is a compact adjectival phrase: mal (an abstract noun meaning illness/harm) + del (di + il) + corpo intero (‘whole body’) means ‘sick in his entire body’ or ‘ill in his whole body.’ Word order (mal + del + noun) is typical of older Italian where mal functions like an adjective-noun unit.
segments.purgatorio-18-g134: e de la mente peggio, continues the description in parallel: e (and) + de la (archaic form of di + la) + mente (mind) + peggio (comparative/adverb ‘worse’). The construction says he is worse in mind than in body; the older form de la instead of della is a poetic archaism.
segments.purgatorio-18-g135: e che mal nacque, is a brief relative clause: e che (‘and who/and that’) introduces the additional negative quality, mal nacque is perfect (passé) of nascere used in narrative to mean ‘he was born ill/ill-born’ or ‘born badly.’ The verb nacque is simple past (passato remoto) and the phrase is an ironical moral judgement about his origins.
segments.purgatorio-18-g136: ha posto in loco di suo pastor vero». is the main verb clause explaining the consequence: ha posto (present perfect: ha + past participle posto) means ‘has placed’ or ‘has set’ and takes as direct object the prepositional complement in loco (‘in the place/post’) with di suo pastor vero (‘of its true shepherd’). Pastor vero is a nominal phrase ‘true pastor/shepherd’ — the role that the ill-qualified person now occupies.
segments.purgatorio-18-g137: Io non so se più disse o s’ei si tacque, is a coordinate interrogative/indirect question: Io non so (‘I do not know’) + se (‘whether’) introduces the alternative. Più disse (‘said more’) vs. s’ei si tacque is an archaic clitic combination: s’ei = se ei (if he) and si tacque is reflexive perfect ‘fell silent.’ Tacque is passato remoto (he fell silent). The clause contrasts continuation of speech with sudden silence.
segments.purgatorio-18-g138: tant’ era già di là da noi trascorso; tant’ is a poetic truncation of tanto (‘so much/so far’), followed by era già (imperfect + already) meaning ‘he was already’ and di là da noi trascorso literally ‘passed over to the other side from us’ i.e. ‘had gone away from us.’ Trascorso is past participle used intransitively here to indicate movement/placement in distance; the order emphasizes how far he had moved off.
segments.purgatorio-18-g139: ma questo intesi, is a short declarative: ma (‘but’) contrasts with the preceding uncertainty, questo (‘this’) is the direct object and intesi is passato remoto of intendere which here means ‘I heard/learned.’ In older Italian intendere frequently carries the sense ‘to hear’ in narrative contexts.
segments.purgatorio-18-g140: e ritener mi piacque. joins two infinitival constructions: ritener (infinitive ‘to retain/keep in mind’) is the action, mi is the indirect object ‘to me,’ and piacque is passato remoto of piacere ‘it pleased’ — literally ‘it pleased me to retain it,’ i.e. ‘I wished to keep it in memory.’ The syntax follows the impersonal construction typical of piacere.
segments.purgatorio-18-g141: E quei che m’era ad ogne uopo soccorso disse: opens with E (‘and’) then quei che (‘he who’) as the subject; m’era (mi + era) is an impersonal-like construction meaning ‘was to me’ or ‘was for me,’ indicating that this person had been a helper; ad ogne uopo is an archaic phrase ‘for every need,’ soccorso is a substantive meaning ‘aid/help.’ Disse is the reporting verb ‘said.’
segments.purgatorio-18-g142: «Volgiti qua: is a direct imperative addressed to the narrator: Volgiti is the reflexive imperative of volgere/volgersi (‘turn yourself’ or ‘turn around’), and qua (‘here/hither’) indicates the direction. The punctuation (colon) introduces the demonstrative imperative clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-18-g143: vedine due venir dando a l’accidïa di morso». contains an imperative verb with enclitic pronoun: vedine is the poetic contraction of vedi + ne meaning ‘see (of them)/look — there are two of them’; due (‘two’) is the direct object; venir (infinitive) follows in a periphrastic construction ‘see two of them coming’; dando (gerund of dare) 'giving' modifies the coming and l’accidïa di morso is a figurative phrase ‘to acedia (sloth) a bite’ meaning they give sloth a sting/bite. Note vedine is archaic/poetic and here should be parsed as an imperative + clitic.
segments.purgatorio-18-g144: Di retro a tutti dicean: places the speaker of the following words: Di retro (‘from the rear/behind’) + a tutti (‘to all/everyone’) and dicean is imperfect (poetic) ‘they were saying’—a collective comment made by the crowd behind. Word order (prepositional phrase before verb) stresses the location/source of the speech.
segments.purgatorio-18-g145: «Prima fue morta la gente a cui il mar s’aperse, uses Prima fue morta (archaic passato: ‘first was dead/died’) to mean ‘first died’ or ‘died before.’ La gente a cui (‘the people to whom’) introduces a relative clause; il mar s’aperse is a pronominal/passive reflexive aperirsi ‘the sea opened itself’ (i.e. the sea parted). The tense and word order reflect older, poetic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-18-g146: che vedesse Iordan le rede sue. is a subordinate clause introduced by che (‘that/before’) with vedesse (imperfect subjunctive here used in a comparative temporal sense: ‘before [he/it] saw’ or ‘that... might see’). Iordan is the river Jordan; le rede sue (rede = nets in medieval usage) means ‘his/their nets’ — the clause says the events occurred before Jordan saw its nets. The subjunctive is used poetically for the earlier-than relation.
segments.purgatorio-18-g41: Or = 'now'; ti is the dative 'to you'. puote is an archaic/poetic present of potere ('can/may'); apparer is the infinitive 'to appear' so puote apparer = 'may/can appear'. quant' è nascosa is an exclamatory/relative phrase 'how hidden (it) is' with the finite verb è (is) and nascosa (past participial adjective 'hidden') agreeing with la veritate. The phrase a la gente is a dative/indirect-argument (literally 'to the people'), and ch’avvera is a relative clause 'who/which make/bring about' introducing the clause that follows; note the inversion and condensed word order typical of Dante.
segments.purgatorio-18-g42: però che here introduces a reason/explanation (often 'for/because' in Dante). forse appar = 'perhaps it appears/seems' (appar = appear/seem). la sua matera = 'its matter' (sua refers back to the love or to the thing in question). esser buona is the infinitive construction after appar (apparer esser = 'appear to be'); sempre emphasises 'always/ever'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g43: ma non ciascun segno è buono = 'but not every sign/indication is good.' Note the simple negative (non) plus predicate adjective buono; ciascun stresses 'each/ every single' sign.
segments.purgatorio-18-g44: ancor che buona sia la cera is a concessive clause introduced by ancor che (= although/even though): ancòr che + subjunctive (sia) = 'even though the wax may be good.' sia is the present subjunctive of essere, used here after a concessive conjunction; cera ('wax') is the image (the material substrate) whose goodness does not guarantee the goodness of every sign.
segments.purgatorio-18-g45: A simple nominal phrase: Le tue parole = 'your words'; e + ’l mio seguace ingegno = 'and my following/attendant intellect/wit.' seguace functions adjectivally ('attendant'/'following') modifying ingegno; note the definite article ’l before mio, a normal poetic feature.
segments.purgatorio-18-g46: rispuos' io lui is an inversion typical of Dante: rispuos' is a contracted/poetic past form of risposi (I replied); io is the nominative subject; lui is dative/indirect object ('to him'). The concluding comma marks the reported speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-18-g47: m’hanno amor discoverto is a compound perfect construction: m' = mi (me), hanno = auxiliary 'have', discoverto = past participle ('discovered/revealed'). Word order is compressed: literally 'have revealed love to me' (love is what has been revealed to me). discoverto is an older form of scoperto/discoverto.
segments.purgatorio-18-g48: ma ciò m’ha fatto di dubbiar più pregno = 'but that has made me more pregnant/full of doubt.' ciò = 'that/this', m' = 'me', ha fatto = 'has made', di + infinitive/noun construction (di dubbiar) expresses 'full of doubt' where pregno ('pregnant/full of') takes a complement introduced by di.
segments.purgatorio-18-g49: ché = 'for/because' introducing the explanatory clause that follows; very common in Dante to introduce a causal clause.
segments.purgatorio-18-g50: s’amore è di fuori a noi offerto = 'if love is offered to us from outside.' s’ is the contraction of se ('if'); amore è offerto = 'love is offered' with di fuori 'from outside' and a noi marking the recipients. Offerto is a past participle used adjectivally/predicatively.
segments.purgatorio-18-g51: se dritta o torta va = 'whether it goes straight or crooked.' se here = 'whether/if' (introducing the alternative), dritta/torta are adjectives agreeing (feminine) with anima implicit in the previous clause; va is 3rd-person singular 'goes'.
segments.purgatorio-18-g52: non è suo merto = 'it is not its merit.' Non + è is the negated copula; suo refers back to l’anima (its merit), and merto is an archaic form meaning 'merit/desert.' The clause closes the conditional reasoning: if the soul follows an externally offered love, the outcome is not the soul's own merit.
segments.purgatorio-18-g53: Ed elli a me: = 'And he to me:' — Ed is a variant of e ('and'); elli is the subject 'he' (here used in the singular), followed by the dative a me ('to me'), marking direct address/introduction to the reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-18-g54: Quanto ragion qui vede, = 'As much reasoning as you/one see(s) here' — quanto introduces a measure ('as much as'), ragion = 'reasoning/argument', qui 'here', vede is 3rd-person singular of vedere; in this elliptical phrase vede is interpreted with reference to what 'you' (or one) perceives here.
segments.purgatorio-18-g55: dir ti poss’ io; = 'I can tell you' but with inverted order: dir (infinitive) + ti (to you) + poss' (contracted posso = 'can') + io (subject). The poetic contraction poss' = posso and the placing of infinitive first is characteristic of Dante's style.
segments.purgatorio-18-g56: da indi in là t’aspetta pur a Beatrice = 'from here on, await only Beatrice' — da indi in là = 'from this point onward'; t’ aspetta = 'await/expect you' (t' = ti), pur = 'only/still' adding emphasis, a Beatrice = 'Beatrice' as the person to whom he should look.
segments.purgatorio-18-g57: ch’è opra di fede. = 'who/which is a work of faith.' ch’ = che 'who/which', è = 'is', opra = 'work/act', di fede = 'of faith' (i.e. belonging to or requiring faith). The phrase gives a theological qualifier for Beatrice's role.
segments.purgatorio-18-g58: Ogne forma sustanzïal = 'Every substantial (essential) form.' Ogne = ogni (every), forma = form, sustanzïal (substantial) is an adjective emphasizing the metaphysical/essential nature of the form.
segments.purgatorio-18-g59: che setta è da matera ed è con lei unita = 'which is set by matter and is united with it.' che introduces the relative clause. setta is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'is set/determined' (passive sense), da matera = 'by matter', ed è con lei unita = 'and is united with it' (lei = matter).
segments.purgatorio-18-g60: specifica vertute ha in sé colletta = 'it has a specific virtue collected in itself.' specifica vertute = 'a specific/characteristic power/virtue'; ha is the verb 'has'; in sé = 'in itself'; colletta (literally 'collected') indicates the virtue is gathered/contained in the form itself.
segments.purgatorio-18-g61: la qual sanza operar non è sentita = 'which, without operating, is not perceived.' la qual refers to the specific virtue; sanza (archaic for senza) = 'without'; operar = 'operation/acting' (infinitive used as noun-like), non è sentita = 'is not felt/sensed.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g62: né si dimostra mai che per effetto = 'nor is it ever shown except/only through effect.' né ... mai = 'nor ... ever'; si is the impersonal/reflexive pronoun used to form a passive/impersonal ('is shown'); dimostra = 'shows/proves'; che per effetto introduces the limitation 'except by/through effect.'
segments.purgatorio-18-g63: come per verdi fronde in pianta vita = 'as (is) by green leaves in a living plant.' come per = 'as by/through', verdi fronde = 'green leaves' (fronde = leaves), in pianta vita = 'in a plant that is alive' — the image shows that the form's virtue is perceptible through its visible effects (the green leaves of a living plant).

sentences.purgatorio-18-s1: The lofty doctor had put an end to his argument, and attentively watched my face to see whether I seemed content; and I, to whom a new thirst still searched within, was silent outwardly, and inside said: 'Perhaps the asking too much that I make burdens them.'
sentences.purgatorio-18-s2: But that truthful father, who perceived the timid will that would not reveal itself, speaking, offered me the boldness to speak.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s3: Whereupon I said: 'Master, my sight is so revived in your light that I discern clearly how far your reasoning departs or describes.'
sentences.purgatorio-18-s4: Therefore I beg you, sweet dear father, that you show me Love, to whom you reduce every good action and its contrary.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s5: 'Direct (them),' he said, 'turn to me the keen lights of the intellect, and the error of the blind who make themselves leaders will be made manifest to you.'
sentences.purgatorio-18-s6: The soul, which is created to love quickly, is movable toward every thing that pleases, as soon as it is roused from pleasure into action.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s7: Your apprehension, taking itself to be true, draws intention and unfolds it within you, so that the soul is caused to turn toward it; and if, turned, it inclines toward her, that inclining is love — that is the nature which, through renewed pleasure, is bound in you.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s8: Then, as fire moves upward because of its nature — born to climb to those places where its matter is harder — so the soul, once seized, enters into desire, which is a spiritual motion and never rests until the thing loved makes it rejoice.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s17: Therefore, let us suppose that every love which is kindled within you arises from necessity; it is within your power to restrain it.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s18: Beatrice's noble virtue understands [this] as concerning free will; and therefore take care to keep that in mind, should she take up to speak.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s19: The moon, as if tardy at the middle of the night, made the stars seem to us more sparse, like a bookworm who is always burning; and she ran across the sky along those paths which the sun sets alight when, seen from Rome between Sardinia and Corsica, it is seen to fall.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s20: And that gentle shade—by whom one is called 'Pietola' rather than the Mantuan villa—had taken the burden off my prison; so I, who had gathered clear and plain reason about my questions, stood like a man whose drowsiness is in vain.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s33: And she who did not endure the anguish to the end with Anchises' son offered herself to a life without glory.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s34: Then, when those shades were so far from us that they could be seen no more, a new thought entered into me, from which others — many and different — were born; and I so fancied from one thing to another that, my eyes overcome by longing, I closed them, and I changed my thought into a dream.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s13: Therefore, from whence the intellect of the first notions comes, man did not know, nor the feeling for the first desirable things, which are in you as the bee’s industry in making honey; and this first desire can receive no merit of praise or of blame.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s14: Now, because every other [desire] gathers to this, an innate virtue is in you that counsels, and (there is) the consent to keep the boundary/threshold.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s15: This is the principle from which is taken the reason for deserving in you, according as it welcomes and watches over good and guilty loves.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s16: Those who, reasoning, went to the bottom perceived this innate liberty; therefore they left morality to the world.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s25: O people in whom a keen fervor now perhaps fills in for the negligence and delay on your part caused by lukewarmness in doing good, this one who is alive — and certainly I do not lie to you — wants to go up, provided the sun shines on him; therefore tell us where the opening is nearby.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s26: These were the words of my duke; and one of those spirits said: 'Come behind us, and you will find the hole.'
sentences.purgatorio-18-s27: We are so full of desire to move ourselves that we cannot stay; therefore forgive us, if you regard our rudeness as justice.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s28: I was abbot in San Zeno at Verona under the empire of the good Barbarossa, of whom a sorrowful Milan still speaks.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s21: But this drowsiness was suddenly taken from me by people who had already turned their backs toward us.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s22: And such as Ismenus and Asopus once saw—along their banks at night—fury and a press; whenever the Thebans had need of Bacchus, such a throng makes its passage through that circle of his, from what I saw of those people coming, on whom good will and righteous love ride.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s23: Soon they were over us, for that whole great crowd was in motion as it ran; and two in front were crying out, weeping: "Mary hurried to the mountain; and Caesar, to subdue Ilerda, struck Marseille and then rushed into Spain."
sentences.purgatorio-18-s24: "Quick, quick, so that time not be lost through small love," the others cried after them, "for zeal in doing good renews grace."
sentences.purgatorio-18-s29: And such a one already has one foot inside the pit, so that that monastery will soon lament, and will be sorry to have held power; for its son, sick in his whole body and worse in mind, and who was ill-born, has placed himself in the post of its true pastor.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s30: I do not know whether he said more or whether he fell silent, for he had already gone so far away from us; but this I heard, and I wished to keep it in memory.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s31: And he who had been my help on every occasion said: “Turn here; see two of them coming, giving a bite to sloth.”
sentences.purgatorio-18-s32: From behind they all were saying: “First the people died to whom the sea opened itself, before Jordan could see its nets.”
sentences.purgatorio-18-s9: Now it may appear to you how hidden the truth is from the people who make each love in itself a praiseworthy thing.
sentences.purgatorio-18-s10: "Your words and my attending wit," I answered him, "have revealed love to me; but that has made me more full of doubt; for, if love is offered to us from outside and the soul does not go with another foot, whether it goes straight or crooked, it is not its merit."
sentences.purgatorio-18-s11: And he to me: "As much reasoning as you see here I can tell you; from here on, await only Beatrice, who is a work of faith."
sentences.purgatorio-18-s12: Every substantial form, which is set by matter and is united with it, has a specific virtue gathered in itself, which without operating is not perceived, nor is it ever shown except by effect, as (is) by green leaves in a living plant.
